author: George Orwell
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "1984" book by George Orwell?
Options:
A. The two of them did not speak again after that interaction. She walked at a speed that prevented him from keeping up with her, without making an obvious attempt to lose him. He had intended to accompany her to the subway station, but suddenly trailing after her in the cold seemed meaningless and unbearable. More than wanting to get away from Julia, he felt a powerful urge to return to the Chestnut Tree Cafe, which was very appealing to him in that moment. He had a nostalgic image in his mind of his usual table there, with the newspaper, chessboard, and constantly flowing gin.
B. They fell silent following that exchange. She proceeded at a pace that made it difficult for him to stay beside her, though she did not try to shake him off outright. He had resolved to go with her all the way to the subway stop, but this act of plodding along behind her in the cold now seemed senseless and intolerable. Overwhelming his desire to distance himself from Julia was a longing to be back at the Chestnut Tree Cafe, which had never before seemed so welcoming. He pictured fondly his customary table there, with the newspaper, chess set, and bottomless gin.
C. They stopped talking to each other after that. She walked at a pace that made it hard for him to walk beside her, though she did not overtly try to shake him off. He was determined to go with her all the way to the subway station, but following her in the cold like that now seemed pointless and intolerable. More than wanting to get away from Julia, he felt an overwhelming desire to be back at the Chestnut Tree Cafe, which had never before seemed so welcoming. He pictured his corner table there fondly, with the newspaper, chess set, and endless gin.
D. They did not speak again. She did not actually try to shake him off, but walked at just such a speed as to prevent his keeping abreast of her. He had made up his mind that he would accompany her as far as the Tube station, but suddenly this process of trailing along in the cold seemed pointless and unbearable. He was overwhelmed by a desire not so much to get away from Julia as to get back to the Chestnut Tree Cafe, which had never seemed so attractive as at this moment. He had a nostalgic vision of his corner table, with the newspaper and the chessboard and the ever-flowing gin.
Answer:

options.A: The two of them did not speak again after that interaction. She walked at a speed that prevented him from keeping up with her, without making an obvious attempt to lose him. He had intended to accompany her to the subway station, but suddenly trailing after her in the cold seemed meaningless and unbearable. More than wanting to get away from Julia, he felt a powerful urge to return to the Chestnut Tree Cafe, which was very appealing to him in that moment. He had a nostalgic image in his mind of his usual table there, with the newspaper, chessboard, and constantly flowing gin.

He had resolved to go with her all the way to the subway stop, but this act of plodding along behind her in the cold now seemed senseless and intolerable. He pictured fondly his customary table there, with the newspaper, chess set, and bottomless gin.

More than wanting to get away from Julia, he felt an overwhelming desire to be back at the Chestnut Tree Cafe, which had never before seemed so welcoming. He pictured his corner table there fondly, with the newspaper, chess set, and endless gin.
options.D: They did not speak again. She did not actually try to shake him off, but walked at just such a speed as to prevent his keeping abreast of her. He had made up his mind that he would accompany her as far as the Tube station, but suddenly this process of trailing along in the cold seemed pointless and unbearable. He was overwhelmed by a desire not so much to get away from Julia as to get back to the Chestnut Tree Cafe, which had never seemed so attractive as at this moment.
